title: Exposition of the Pater Noster
summary: The checked-in CME00111 XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00111, which encodes Frank A. Patterson's complete 1916 article 'A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer' from The Journal of English and Germanic Philology 15, pp. 406-418. Patterson directly transcribed Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r (his f. 186a-196a), adding one introductory paragraph, two notes, and a signed closer. The encoded medieval poem has 596 verse-line elements in five groups plus a terminal Amen closer; Patterson accidentally prints marginal line number 90 twice, so later displayed numbers are five below XML position. DIMEV 1573 / IMEV 958 identifies Bodleian MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, as the only other indexed witness; it is collateral, not a source of Patterson's transcription. Complete public edition, OCR, metadata, catalogue, and data-release objects are cached where available. No complete public facsimile of either manuscript was found in the dated search scope: Cambridge supplies a current whole-object imaging request route, while the only public Douce image object has two slides, f. 1r and f. 139r, and does not include the work.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00111
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00111; the file is 31,950 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00111
entities.description: Public digital text of Patterson's complete thirteen-page article. It retains the article heading and prose introduction, printed pages 406-418, manuscript folio milestones, poem, marginal line numbers, two notes, italic typography, terminal Amen, and Patterson's signature and affiliation.

entities.label: Exposition of the Pater Noster (DIMEV 1573 / IMEV 958)
alternate_labels: God of heaven that sittest in throne
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English aa-couplet exposition of the Lord's Prayer represented by two indexed witnesses. This abstract work layer is distinct from either physical codex, Patterson's Cambridge-witness transcription, and the U-M encoding.

entities.label: Frank A. Patterson, A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer (1916)
entities.description: Patterson's complete article consists of a short modern introduction and direct transcription of CUL Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r, with folio and line milestones, two notes, italic typography, a terminal Amen, and a signed closer. No editorial-method statement explains every italic span.

bibliographic.citation: Frank A. Patterson, 'A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer,' The Journal of English and Germanic Philology 15 (Urbana, Ill.: University of Illinois, 1916), 406-418.
bibliographic.title: A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer
bibliographic.container_title: The Journal of English and Germanic Philology

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89
entities.description: A parchment codex of i + 196 + i leaves, approximately 7.75 x 5.25 inches, written in several clear hands in one column. The target poem is in another hand from the preceding texts, begins on f. 186r, ends on f. 196r, and leaves f. 196v blank. James and Pink date the codex late fourteenth century; Patterson allows early fifteenth or possibly late fourteenth.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Late fourteenth century in James and Pink; early fifteenth or possibly late fourteenth in Patterson

holding.notes: The Apollo Pink and James datasets are official repository publications of historical unpublished descriptions. Cambridge's current search guidance says many medieval manuscripts still rely on printed catalogues and directs questions to Special Collections.

physical_description.format: Codex; single column; James reports 32 lines per page, Pink 26-34
notes: James describes three or four clear, rather rough hands; the target is introduced as 'Another hand.'
Pink records several late-fourteenth-century hands, red headings, red and blue paraphs and initials.
Rebound by Douglas Cockerell & Son, Letchworth, in 1963.
Patterson's f. 186a-196a notation is normalized here to ff. 186r-196r; James says 196v is blank.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of JEGP volume 15 (1916)
entities.description: A complete 668-page PDF of volume 15. Patterson's article occupies printed pp. 406-418 / PDF pp. 414-426; complete-volume OCR and exact Internet Archive item metadata are also cached.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete-volume PDF, OCR text, and item metadata

entities.label: H. L. Pink's unpublished description of CUL MS Dd.11.89
entities.description: Two-page handwritten historical description scanned and published by Cambridge University Library in Apollo in 2021. It records the codex's late-fourteenth-century date, contents, physical features, decoration, and binding history.

entities.label: M. R. James's unpublished description of CUL MS Dd.11.89
entities.description: Historical unpublished manuscript description published as PDF and text transcription by Cambridge University Library in Apollo in 2021. It supplies an exact target incipit, explicit, f. 186-196a locus, and the statement that f. 196v is blank.

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 record 1573 (IMEV/NIMEV 958)
entities.description: Versioned scholarly record identifying the poem as an exposition of the Pater Noster in two-line aa verse and listing two witnesses: Bodleian Douce 141, ff. 130-138, and CUL Dd.11.89, ff. 186-196, with Patterson's edition attached to the Cambridge witness.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Douce 141
entities.description: A first-half-fifteenth-century English parchment codex containing the collateral IMEV 958 witness at ff. 130r-138r. The MWM catalogue gives the target incipit and explicit and warns that texts after the two analyzed Prick of Conscience hands have not been linguistically analyzed.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

physical_description.extent: Current Bodleian summary: v + 153 leaves excluding interleaves; MWM describes v + 151 + iii with detailed foliation
physical_description.format: Codex; target in the later contents after the two analyzed Prick of Conscience hands; MWM reports one column of 36-37 lines
physical_description.dimensions: Binding 10 x 6.75 inches; MWM leaf dimensions 246 x 160 mm
notes: MWM describes two- and three-line blue initials with red penwork and a good overall condition.
The target work's own hand and dialect are not assigned by the inspected MWM data.

entities.label: Medieval West Midlands catalogue entry for MS. Douce 141
entities.description: Scholarly TEI description in A Catalogue of Vernacular Manuscript Books of the English West Midlands. It identifies the target as 'Pater Noster: Exposition (IMEV 958)' at ff. 130r-138r and supplies codicological and linguistic-scope cautions.

entities.label: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog record for MS. Douce 141
entities.description: Current official summary record with shelfmark, ARKs, aggregate contents, first-half-fifteenth-century English origin, physical description, ownership history, contact email, and a declared partial digital surrogate of two 35mm slides.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian two-slide object for MS. Douce 141
entities.description: The complete provider-published object contains exactly two images, f. 1r and f. 139r, made from 35mm slides. Neither image overlaps the target ff. 130r-138r, so the object is partial relative to the codex and cannot support direct comparison of this work.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest with two 35mm-slide images

rights.component: Medieval Middle English linguistic and literary content
rights.basis: The anonymous work is attested in manuscripts from the late fourteenth or first half of the fifteenth century and is centuries outside copyright term; modern editions, photographs, catalogues, and markup remain separately scoped.

rights.component: 1916 article text, transcription, editorial notes, and typography

editorial_practices.source_feature: Seventy-seven italic spans within the Middle English verse; Patterson gives no explicit convention statement
editorial_practices.representation: Italic type retained as HI elements in the U-M-derived XML
editorial_practices.description: Many forms appear consistent with editorial expansion of manuscript abbreviations, but without manuscript images or a method statement it is unsafe to classify every italic span as expansion rather than supply, uncertainty, or another function.
editorial_practices.scope: Poem on printed pp. 406-418 / CUL Dd.11.89 ff. 186r-196r
notes: The seventy-eighth HI element is Patterson's modern Columbia University affiliation and is not part of this practice.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Patterson article embedded in a complete journal volume
editorial_practices.representation: One electronic item spanning the complete article from heading through signed closer
editorial_practices.description: The encoding selects Patterson's article from JEGP volume 15 but does not omit known pages within the thirteen-page article.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD, IDG, TEXT/BODY/DIV1 and complete descendants
evidence.summary: Direct local evidence for identifiers, repository artifact, U-M handle, Patterson-derived complete article, medieval poem, modern paratext, page and folio milestones, notes, typography, terminal Amen, and signed closer.

evidence.citation: Reproducible structural and source-boundary audit of CME00111.xml against Patterson's scanned article.
evidence.locator: Element counts, nesting, printed page groups, manuscript folio milestones, line numbers, first and last lines
evidence.summary: Confirms 31,950 bytes; one DIV1; one HEAD; one P; five LG; 596 L; two NOTE; 140 MILESTONE; thirteen PB; two CLOSER; one SIGNED; one LB; 78 HI; complete printed pp. 406-418; duplicated printed line number 90 at XML lines 90 and 95; and final printed 590 at XML line 595 followed by XML line 596.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item CME00111.
evidence.summary: Stable official item identity. Automated access to the name resolver returned HTTP 403 during this review, so content and structure claims are independently grounded in the checked-in XML and scanned edition.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection description and access statement.
evidence.summary: Describes a public corpus manually keyed from public-domain editions, proofread and encoded in basic TEI, and applies No Copyright-United States to collection materials.

evidence.citation: Frank A. Patterson, 'A Sermon on the Lord's Prayer,' The Journal of English and Germanic Philology 15 (1916): 406-418.
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title and authorship, Cambridge shelfmark, Patterson's direct transcription and dating claim, older catalogue citation, exact article and poem boundaries, folio and line milestones, notes, italic typography, duplicated line number 90, terminal Amen, and signed closer. The volume title page establishes 1916 despite Internet Archive's generic 1897 metadata date.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive, The Journal of English and Germanic Philology volume 15, item journalofenglish15urbauoft.

Exact item metadata applies CC BY 4.0.

This establishes only no public copy found in the checked scope, not nondigitization.

The OTA item applies CC BY-NC-SA 3.0.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF Presentation manifest and complete-bundle audit for MS. Douce 141 object aa5d89e9-b871-4696-a44b-56753db9bec8.

Current contextual provider terms linked from the exact two-slide object's attribution. Any separately supplied complete images require their own order-specific terms.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.summary: Official duration overview supporting United States public-domain treatment of Patterson's 1916 publication; access-provider terms and other jurisdictions remain separate.

open_questions.question: What exact Cambridge manuscript features do Patterson's 77 italic spans inside the medieval poem represent?
next_steps: Obtain complete page images of CUL Dd.11.89 and compare every span against ff. 186r-196r.
Classify abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and any other editorial functions separately.
Retain the edition's italic signal without silently normalizing it until direct evidence resolves each span.

open_questions.question: Has CUL Dd.11.89 been digitized internally, and can a complete color facsimile be supplied with usable research and publication terms?
next_steps: Submit a whole-codex request through Cambridge Imaging Services and identify ff. 186r-196r separately.
Ask whether existing internal photography or microfilm can reduce cost and whether covers, endleaves, blanks, every leaf, and edges can be included.
Obtain separate written terms for private research, repository redistribution, scholarly illustration, and commercial or print-on-demand use before publishing any supplied image.
notes: No complete public copy was found in the checked scope; that does not mean the manuscript has never been digitized.

open_questions.question: Can a complete facsimile of MS. Douce 141 be obtained, and what hand and dialect copied the IMEV 958 text on ff. 130r-138r?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections about complete existing photography, microfilm, or a whole-codex imaging order and exact reuse terms.
Map ff. 130r-138r within any complete supplied object; do not substitute the public f. 139r slide, which begins after the target.
Perform or locate target-hand linguistic analysis rather than applying the Warwickshire and Oxfordshire labels for the preceding Prick of Conscience hands.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition date and place be established more narrowly than by the late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century Cambridge attestation in England?
next_steps: Consult work-specific scholarship and collate both witnesses.
Keep Cambridge manuscript dating, DIMEV's codex-level East Anglia label, Douce production, and abstract-work composition as separate claims.
Reassess whether James/Pink's late-fourteenth-century dating or Patterson's broader alternative is favored by current codicology.

review_notes: The primary transmission path is repository XML to U-M encoding to Patterson's 1916 edition to CUL Dd.11.89, ff. 186r-196r.
Bodleian MS. Douce 141, ff. 130r-138r, is the only other DIMEV witness and is collateral. No relation says Patterson transcribed or collated it.
Patterson's f. 186a-196a notation is normalized to ff. 186r-196r because James explicitly places the ending on 196a and says 196v is blank.

The encoded item contains the complete article, including one modern prose introduction and Patterson's signed closer. Those paratexts are retained in source accounting but do not make the medieval work prose, mixed-form, modern English, or Patterson-authored.

The XML contains 596 medieval verse L elements plus a terminal Amen closer. Patterson prints marginal line number 90 twice; all later displayed numbers are five behind XML position, so displayed 590 accompanies XML line 595 and the final verse line is unnumbered.

Seventy-seven HI elements occur in the poem and one in Patterson's affiliation. The likely expansion function of poem italics remains unverified without manuscript images and is recorded at low confidence.

No complete public facsimile of CUL Dd.11.89 or MS. Douce 141 was found in the dated search scope. Cambridge offers a current paid online imaging route; the Bodleian catalog provides a Special Collections contact. Neither negative search is an assertion of nondigitization.

The complete public two-slide Digital Bodleian object is cached, but its only images are f. 1r and f. 139r, so it has no target-work portion. Complete editions, OCR, catalogues, and versioned data releases are cached whole under the ignored source-cache/CME00111 directory.

Rights remain layer-specific: the medieval work and 1916 article are public domain in the United States; U-M applies NoC-US; Cambridge description datasets are CC BY 4.0; DIMEV is CC BY 4.0; MWM is CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; the two Bodleian slides are CC BY-NC 4.0; future supplied manuscript images require their exact order terms.
